1: Your Wisdom stat gives you more time to answer quiz questions. Every point above 10 adds a second.
Stamina (SP) drains as you move. When it runs low, you'll grow Hungry — and eventually start losing HP.
Scrolls are consumed when read, but spellbooks can teach you a spell to cast forever.
Cooked food restores far more than eating things raw. A wise adventurer learns to cook early.
Pressing ? opens the help screen, which lists every key command in the game.
Lockpicks wear out with use. A fragile pick breaks faster when you fail to open a lock.
Gold and items are sometimes hidden inside locked chests — watch for them in dungeon rooms.
You can pick up items by standing on them and pressing G or comma.
The dungeon gets harder the deeper you go, but better equipment and ingredients appear too.
Different weapons deal different damage types — slash, pierce, or blunt. Experiment to find what works.
Some say the dungeon itself is a kind of program — and all programs have hidden inputs. Not every key on your keyboard does what you'd expect. Try the ones that seem to do nothing.
2: Fire creatures resist fire damage and are weak to cold. The reverse is also true for ice creatures.
Your Perception (PER) stat increases both your sight radius and the range of ranged weapons.
When confused, your quiz timer is shortened — cure confusion quickly before a fight.
Intelligence (INT) raises your MP cap and gives bonus seconds on magic-subject quizzes.
Cooking monster ingredients gives SP and HP. The deeper the dungeon the monster came from, the better the meal.
Staircase rest heals you each floor — save potions and healing herbs for when stairs are far away.
Undead creatures often resist physical damage but are weak to fire or holy effects.
Weapons with reach can attack monsters one or two tiles away — useful against dangerous melee fighters.
Wands have limited charges — use them wisely against powerful monsters or in desperate situations.
Accessories provide passive effects when equipped. Some grant elemental resistances.
Strength (STR) improves both your carrying capacity and your maximum stamina pool.
Constitution (CON) directly raises your maximum HP. Investing in CON makes you much harder to kill.

Philosophers speak of a reality beneath reality — a hidden terminal that accepts a spoken word. The key to reach it is not listed in any help screen. It sits beside the number 1, quiet and overlooked.
Strange altars sometimes appear in the dungeon. Those who approach and interact with them discover ancient challenges — and ancient rewards.
Polearms and staves can strike enemies beyond arm's reach. A spearman need not stand toe-to-toe with a beast to draw blood.
3: Wolf-type ingredients combined with deep cave fungi are said to produce meals of considerable strength.
Two healing ingredients combined in a recipe create a restorative meal more powerful than either alone.
Dragon-type ingredients, when carefully cooked, produce meals that greatly increase physical resilience.
Ingredients from flying creatures combined with herbs often create meals that sharpen the mind.
Bone-based ingredients combined with cave mushrooms are said to fortify the body's endurance.
Undead-derived ingredients, when properly prepared, can grant temporary supernatural resistances.
The deeper the dungeon, the stronger the recipe ingredients. A tier-3 recipe with tier-5 parts is exceptionally powerful.
Some compound recipes require three or more ingredients — experiment to discover the most powerful combinations.
Aquatic creature ingredients combined with underground plants can create meals that clear the mind.
Fire-type and cold-type ingredients balanced together create a meal said to improve all statistics equally.
A fire-bringer in a starlit robe, sashed in gold, is said to walk as a hidden character — his endurance is remarkable and his legacy burns eternal.
One hidden character owns almost nothing: bald, simply robed, and unfazed by hardship. His wisdom is legendary because he demands so little of the world.
A cunning wanderer in a hood, said to have survived ten years of misadventure at sea, begins the dungeon with exceptional intellect and keen perception.
Two female spellcasters share the same face but differ in arcane mastery. Both begin with a wand and a spellbook — one learned magic, the other mastered it.
A philosopher-mathematician in forest green, with a love of numbers and harmonics, is said to be a hidden character with a brilliantly sharp intellect.
There are rare, powerful creatures that don't follow the dungeon's normal rules — they appear only once per descent, if at all. Treat their appearance as both a warning and an opportunity.
Diogenes carried nothing but a lantern and his convictions. Those who willingly walk in darkness sometimes find wisdom in what they gave up.
A spider of unusual size and cunning lurks in the early depths. Her web immobilizes even armored warriors — but webs have a well-known enemy.
Something half-woman and half-serpent has been reported in the depths — she drains the warmth from those she embraces.
A bronze giant walks slowly through certain levels, shrugging off every arrow and blade. Travelers who survived noticed a curious weakness in its construction.
A serpent-woman who mothers all monsters poisons with every bite. Those who have faced her and lived wore protection.
Reports of a forest spirit that lures wanderers to their doom — old stories suggest cold iron disturbs such beings. Do not follow the music.
A bat-god from the underworld has been seen blinding adventurers with its wings. Conventional arms seem to pass through it.
A lone sphinx in the depths poses riddles to those bold enough to listen. The wise know what subject she favors.
Ancient Greek smiths spoke of an unfinished hammer buried deep. Where thunder echoes, dwarven work awaits.
Alchemists sought the power to turn lead to gold. Their crucibles still burn in the shallower depths.
A Celtic cauldron of transformation is said to demand meals as tribute. Cook well before you seek it.
Pandora's box is said to lie somewhere in the depths. But was opening it truly a mistake?
The Wandering Jew walked forever. Those who have walked as far are said to see what others cannot.
Cerberus guarded the underworld's gate. Those who pass through enough doors earn his grudging respect.
The Sphinx was defeated not by strength but by answering while blind to all else.
Musashi's greatest duels were won with restraint. One strike, not many, showed his mastery.
Rasputin survived poison, bullet, and blade before finally falling. Those who dance with death often enough grow harder to kill.
Cassandra's prophecies were true even when her methods seemed flawed. Even wrong answers can lead to right outcomes.
Tantalus was punished by eternal hunger. Those who persist through repeated failure find iron resolve in deprivation.
Orpheus walked among the dead with only his music. Sometimes the bravest thing is to stand still.
The Buddha sat in perfect stillness beneath the bodhi tree, unmoved by every threat. Patience in danger is its own reward.
Hephaestus returned to his forge obsessively, perfecting the same piece again and again. Repetition is the smith's meditation.
In 1976, a game was released that hid a secret word deep underground. Those who found it could bend the rules of the world. The dungeon remembers old games.
4: The Minotaur haunts the labyrinth at the 20th depth. He strikes from nowhere and vanishes through the walls. None can corner him in his own maze — but an old myth tells of a thread that once tamed the labyrinth.

The Gorgon waits at the 40th depth. Her gaze freezes all who meet her eyes, and she never stands still. In the old stories, heroes found ways to fight without looking — or to turn her own power against her.
The Grey Sisters share a single eye between three. It has been spotted in the floors before the Gorgon's domain. Perseus once stole it — and traded it for something far more valuable.
The dragon Fafnir guards the 60th depth. His scales are nearly impenetrable — no element, no enchantment seems to help. But in the old tales, Sigurd struck from below, where the scales are softest.
The great wolf Fenrir prowls near the 80th depth. Survivors describe a creature of terrible endurance. Even the gods struggled to bind him.

Those who have fought Fenrir and lived say the same thing: he grew stronger with every passing moment. By the end, his jaws could swallow the sky. Speed alone will not save you — you must find a way to calm the beast.

The dwarves of Svartalfheim forged Gleipnir from six things that cannot exist. Adventurers have found strange objects in unusual rooms between the dragon's lair and the wolf's den — things that shouldn't be real.
A forge of dwarven make has been discovered in the deep floors before Fenrir's hall. The dwarves built things from impossible parts. Perhaps their forge still remembers how.
Something ancient and terrible waits at the deepest levels. Those who have glimpsed it say conventional weapons are meaningless against it. Only the devout survive.
Bosses tend to occupy unusually large, irregular chambers — rooms that feel different from standard dungeon layouts.
Each boss guards a threshold. Defeating them may not drop treasure immediately, but the passage below opens.
Powerful monsters on deep floors often have specific weaknesses — a careful examination reveals much.
What you cannot see cannot harm you — or so the legend goes. Blindness is usually a curse, but against certain foes it may be a desperate salvation.
A bronze idol of a bull has been found in the floors before the labyrinth. King Minos offended Poseidon by refusing to sacrifice such a bull. Sacred waters remember old debts.
The mightiest of Greek warriors, near-invulnerable, is hidden among the secret characters — golden armor and crimson plume mark an exceptional melee build.
A Spartan warrior who held a narrow pass against overwhelming odds is a hidden character — bronze helm, scarlet cape, and iron constitution define his legend.
A great conqueror in golden crown and royal purple cape, who never lost a battle, is hidden among the classes — a formidable all-around warrior build.
The hero who once slew the Minotaur walks as a hidden character — his helmet and noble lineage mark him as a balanced warrior who feels at home in labyrinths.
A winged divine messenger in silver-blue, gifted with unmatched speed and cunning, is a hidden rogue character — lighter on his feet than any other.
Ancient theology speaks of a second death — one the oldest texts say even Death cannot escape. Revelation may be the beginning of wisdom for those patient enough to observe, gather, and understand.
A riddle-asking guardian has been encountered around level 35. It offers a test before combat — those who pass gain something; those who fail pay for it.
A son of the forge god roams the mid-depths, breathing fire. He shares his father's weakness to the opposite element.
The invulnerable lion of ancient myth appears in the dungeon. Blades and arrows bounce off its hide. Heracles didn't use either.
A Slavic witch with iron teeth heals as she fights. The old stories say iron and flame are what she fears most.
A great serpent juvenile has been reported in the deeper levels. Its venom is legendary. The old tales say only the sky's fury could wound such creatures.
A chaos witch in the mid-depths drains magical power from those she touches. The devout and the fiery have fared best against her.
An Egyptian servant of death drains life itself. It dwells in darkness. The living oppose it with light and faith.
The knights of old sought the Grail in dungeons far below. The worthy must know the ways of faith.
Somewhere below the 30th level, an Oracle offers visions for tribute. Gold opens prophetic eyes.
A sealed tribunal near the 30th depth judges the wise. History holds the answers.
Jason brought back the Fleece by taming beasts, not slaying them. Animal knowledge is the key.
A well exists where wisdom can be purchased, but it demands sacrifice.
Tiresias was blind yet saw more than any sighted prophet. There is power in answering what you cannot see.
Penelope wove and unwove her tapestry to delay her suitors. Patience with equipment rewards the persistent.
The Valkyrie chose who died in battle. She valued precision above all — those who kill from afar honor her.
Merlin learned his craft by experimenting with the unknown. The unidentified wand in your hand might teach you something.
Scheherazade spun tales from books unread. Sometimes you must act before you fully understand.
Crowther and Woods hid a word inside a massive cave. It made no sense to the uninitiated — but it changed everything for those who spoke it.
Paracelsus, father of toxicology, believed that enduring the poison was the path to immunity.
Siegfried bathed in dragon blood. Those who consume what their enemies inflict may gain unexpected resilience.
Job endured every affliction without losing faith. The dungeons hold many kinds of traps — the faithful survive them all.
Asclepius learned healing from serpents. Those who harvest widely from venomous creatures may discover his secret.
The Fisher King's wound could only be healed by the right prayer at the right moment of desperation.
Dionysus saw truth in intoxication. A distorted mind may perceive things a clear one cannot.
Apollo's arrows never missed. Those who achieve perfect chains in battle earn a god's notice.
Athena's owl observed all living things. Knowledge of many creatures is a form of worship.
Loki wore curses like cloaks, changing them freely. Enduring what others discard has its own power.
Thor named his hammer and never changed it. Loyalty to a weapon forges a bond.
Beowulf tore Grendel's arm off barehanded. Sometimes the mightiest weapon is having none at all.
The Norns wove fate from memory and prophecy. Those who seek lore often enough learn to read the threads.
Jormungandr encircled the world, always returning to itself. Some bonds between warrior and weapon deepen through repetition.
Shiva dances the dance of illusion. Those who endure hallucination long enough see through the veil.
Enkidu was born of the wilderness and knew every creature by name. A harvester of many species earns wild wisdom.
Circe transformed her guests through impossible cuisine. Mastering many kinds of cooking is its own form of magic.
Gawain's code demanded he fight even when bleeding. Victory in desperation marks a true knight.
Kali's dance is patient and eternal — she returns to the same foe again and again, never tiring.
The Green Knight picked up his own severed head and walked away. Some survive what should kill them.
Narcissus gazed at his own reflection endlessly. Self-examination, taken far enough, reveals hidden truths.
Persephone mastered the bounty of two worlds. The greatest cooks draw from diverse sources.

A wise adventurer always keeps a healing potion in reserve. The dungeon has a way of presenting situations where only a healer's touch will do — and the cost of having nothing to give can weigh on the soul.

Not every encounter in the dungeon is hostile. Some of the people you meet are simply lost, or hurt, or desperate. What you choose to do when you meet them is between you and whatever judges such things.
5: The Philosopher's Stone lies at the deepest depth. Retrieve it and ascend to claim your victory.
Prayer at a sacred altar carries more weight than prayer elsewhere. The devout grow wiser through communion.
Abaddon resists nearly everything. Those who survived speak of faith as the only weapon that drew blood.
Theseus survived the labyrinth because Ariadne gave him a thread. In the old stories, it all began with a sacrifice at sacred waters — and ended with a gift that revealed every hidden passage.
Perseus defeated the Gorgon with a mirror and a blindfold. The Grey Sisters' Eye was the price he paid for divine aid. Altars remember what is offered to them.

Sigurd slew Fafnir by digging beneath him and striking upward. Before the killing blow, he carried a broken blade — and before the blade was whole, a god had to intervene. The old stories say Odin's methods are not always what you'd expect.
Fenrir's hide turns aside most blows. Enchantment and relentless chains of strikes are the wolf-binders' tools.
The deepest dungeon levels spawn void rations — consuming one before a boss fight sharpens combat reflexes dramatically.
A character who masters both Wisdom and Intelligence can cast powerful spells while enjoying generous quiz timers.
The divine reward the faithful with permanent wisdom. Three boons are available to those who pray well.
Status effects on monsters can be exploited — even a brief opening changes the course of a fight.
The man who catalogued the natural world and tutored a great conqueror is a hidden character — high INT and WIS, scholarly beard, and a blue robe mark his philosophical build.
A bald, bearded philosopher who famously claimed to know nothing is among the hidden characters — his extraordinary Wisdom provides the most generous quiz timers of any build.
A sage who wrote of caves, shadows, and ideal forms walks as a hidden philosopher character — high intellect, purple robe, and a beard that speaks of long contemplation.
A dark-robed philosopher who declared that suffering builds strength walks as a hidden character — his build is austere but psychologically formidable.
A legendary archmage in a star-studded dark robe carries a staff and begins already knowing powerful spells — the most magically gifted of all hidden characters.
A ranger with the keenest eyes of any hidden character walks the dungeon — forest green cloak, a bow instead of a blade, and perception that misses nothing.
An ancient king who stood his ground at a narrow pass would be proud of any warrior who masters the dungeon depths — his secret build favors strength, endurance, and an unyielding spirit.
An arcane archer of sharp mind and steady hand walks as a hidden character — bow in one hand, spellbook in the other, with intellect and dexterity to match any mage or rogue.
The Green Knight regenerates. Wounds close as fast as you can open them — unless something stops the healing entirely.
The great whirlpool beast pulls everything toward its maw. Those who survived say it feared the storm more than any blade.
A severed arm of a demon king strikes three times for every one of yours. It has a weakness to purifying force — and a nasty habit of paralyzing those it touches.
The Wendigo devours stamina itself — you will starve mid-fight if you are not prepared. Old hunters carried provisions and trusted in fire.
The Wild Hunt Captain rides with spectral horsemen. Phantom things fear iron and faith.
Anansi the trickster confuses and relocates his prey. His webs burn. His illusions shatter under force.
A fragment of the World-Serpent carries acid that eats through armor. Ancient remedies and the sky's own power are the only answers.
Perseus held his shield as a mirror. Turning an enemy's power against them is a feat worth repeating.
Theseus mapped every inch of the labyrinth before he struck. Thorough exploration is its own reward.
Hermes moved between worlds faster than thought. Those who master teleportation earn the messenger's favor.
The Sibyl spoke ten thousand prophecies. Early mastery of many questions impresses even the gods.
Ahasverus walked forever and never rested. Those who walk far enough in the dungeon see what others miss.
Ariadne gave her thread so the hero could escape swiftly. Speed through a level is a form of grace.
Morgan le Fay cast her most powerful spells from the edge of ruin. Desperation sharpens magic.
Cu Chulainn's battle-fury was born from fear itself. Some warriors fight best when terrified.
Sisyphus' boulder rests on a steep slope in the deep dungeon. The Fates respect those who bear burdens willingly.
The Fisher King waits to be healed. A rare herb grows near his resting place, and faith is the medicine.
Near the 60th depth, an ancient wounded king waits. The cure grows on his own floor.
Odin hung on the World Tree for nine days to gain wisdom. A similar vigil is said to grant extraordinary sight.
The Mithridates Protocol: survive your own poison, again and again, until it cannot touch you.
Prometheus burned forever and yet lived. Those who endure bleeding without dying grow stronger for it.
Ragnarok's survivor reached the bottom with almost nothing left. Sometimes arriving is enough.
The First Magic Word was said to tear reality apart and send the speaker elsewhere. Some say speaking it still works — if you know where to type it.
Adventurers sometimes tell of useless scraps of leather they find in the dungeon. But are they really useless?
